flashcards.front: What is the Weighted Interval Scheduling Problem (WISP)?
flashcards.back: A problem where we select a subset of non-overlapping intervals to maximize the total weight/profit, given that each interval has an associated weight.

flashcards.front: What is p(i) in Weighted Interval Scheduling?
flashcards.back: p(i) is the index of the rightmost interval that finishes before interval i starts. It is used to determine the compatible previous interval.

flashcards.front: What is the recurrence relation for WISP DP solution?
flashcards.back: DP[i] = max(DP[i-1], weight[i] + DP[p(i)]) where DP[i] represents the maximum weight achievable considering intervals up to i.

flashcards.front: Why must intervals be sorted by finish time in WISP?
flashcards.back: Sorting by finish time ensures that p(i) can be computed efficiently using binary search and maintains the optimal substructure property.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Weighted Interval Scheduling using dynamic programming?
flashcards.back: O(n log n) - O(n log n) for sorting + O(n log n) for computing p(i) using binary search + O(n) for DP computation.

flashcards.front: How do you find p(i) efficiently?
flashcards.back: Using binary search on the sorted finish times. For interval i, find the rightmost interval with finish time <= start time of i.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of WISP DP solution?
flashcards.back: O(n) - we maintain a DP array of size n+1 to store optimal weights for each prefix.

flashcards.front: How does WISP differ from unweighted interval scheduling?
flashcards.back: In unweighted, we maximize number of intervals; in weighted, we maximize sum of weights. Both use similar DP approach but weighted version considers profit values.

flashcards.front: What is the base case in WISP DP?
flashcards.back: DP[0] = 0 (no intervals selected yields zero weight). For 1-indexed arrays, DP[0] represents the case with no intervals.

flashcards.front: Can WISP be solved using memoization?
flashcards.back: Yes, a top-down recursive approach with memoization can also solve WISP with the same time complexity O(n log n).

flashcards.front: What is the greedy choice property in interval scheduling?
flashcards.back: In unweighted case, choosing the interval that finishes earliest works. But in weighted case, greedy by earliest finish does NOT guarantee optimal solution.

flashcards.front: What is the role of binary search in WISP?
flashcards.back: Binary search is used to find p(i) - the predecessor of interval i - in O(log n) time after preprocessing finish times into an array.

flashcards.front: What is the optimal substructure in WISP?
flashcards.back: The optimal solution for first i intervals either includes interval i (plus optimal solution for p(i)) or excludes interval i (optimal for i-1).

flashcards.front: What are real-world applications of WISP?
flashcards.back: Resource allocation, scheduling meetings with different profits, job sequencing with deadlines, airline crew scheduling, room booking systems.

flashcards.front: How do you reconstruct the solution in WISP?
flashcards.back: By backtracking through the DP array: if weight[i] + DP[p(i)] > DP[i-1], include interval i and jump to p(i); otherwise, go to i-1.
